111.reason: [Resetting queue pointers inside loop causes BFS to restart queue for each unvisited component, but since graph is connected, only one component exists. However, after deletion, if vertex 0 is still present, we must start from 0 only. The loop from i=0 to n-1 will attempt BFS on every unvisited node, but problem requires starting from 0 always. So if i>0 and visited[0] is already set, it's okay, but if 0 is unvisited, it starts at 0. However, the bigger issue is that after deletion, if the deleted vertex is marked as visited implicitly by removing its edges, but not skipped in the loop, it might try to BFS on deleted vertex if not handled. But the main logical error is that traversal must always start from vertex 0, not from every unvisited vertex. The loop should only start from 0, not iterate over all vertices.]

126.reason: [When the first edge (head) is the one to delete, q equals p initially, so q->next = p->next effectively does nothing because q is p. This fails to update G[i].link when the first node is the target. Should handle head deletion separately.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [When the first edge (head) is the one to delete, q equals p initially, so q->next = p->next effectively does nothing because q is p. This fails to update G[i].link when the first node is the target. Should handle head deletion separately.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [When the first edge (head) is the one to delete, q is set to head initially, so q->next = p->next would work only if q is the previous node. But when p is the first node (p == G[i].link), then q == p, so q->next = p->next sets G[i].link->next, but doesn't update G[i].link itself. Thus, if the first adjacent vertex is k, it won't be removed from the list because G[i].link still points to the deleted node. This causes the deleted vertex to still appear in adjacency lists, leading to incorrect traversal.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.14285714285714285 , reason:  [When the first edge (p == q == head) matches k, this sets q->next (which is head->next) correctly, but if p is the first node, q is also pointing to head, so this works. However, there's a bug when the first node is the one to delete: the loop initializes p=q=G[i].link, then if p->adj==k, it does q->next = p->next. But since q == p in this case, this sets p->next = p->next, which is harmless, but the head of the list is never updated. So if the first edge is the one to remove, G[i].link still points to the deleted node. This causes dangling pointer or incorrect traversal. The correct approach is to handle head removal separately.]
